VO_EN: The news calls it digital gold.
You're sitting in a cafe somewhere.
The price on the screen keeps climbing.
Somebody behind the counter is talking about blockchain - using the word like it's brand new, like nobody's ever said it before.
You remember writing it into the first line of code.
One million coins sitting in a wallet.
The largest fortune no one has ever moved.
It just sits there in the blockchain waiting for someone who will never come back.
Because the person who owns it doesn't exist.
Then you stood up and walked away.
A screen that hums when it gets warm.
The room smells like old coffee and warm plastic from the monitor.
You've been sitting here so long your back has shaped itself around the chair.
Nobody asked you to fix money.
Nobody asked you to redesign how trust works between strangers who have never met, will never meet, and don't speak the same language.
But the problem keeps you up at night.
Every transaction in the world runs through a middleman.
A company that charges you for the privilege of holding your own money.
You want to remove that someone.
You want to build a system where the math does the trusting.
You write code the way some people pray.
Believing in something no one else can see yet.
You don't eat dinner most nights.
You just keep going until the screen starts to blur, you realize it's 4 AM, and the coffee beside you is cold.
And you still won't have a name.
You title it: Bitcoin - A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System.
You read it one last time before hitting send.
You send it to a cryptography mailing list.
A few dozen people read it.
One replies saying it can't scale.
Another says the energy cost is too high.
You create a profile on the P2P Foundation.
On April 5th, 1933, the US government made it illegal for citizens to own gold.
You picked a birthday that's a protest.
No one to tell when the code compiles at 3 AM and it actually works.
You push back from the desk sometimes and just sit there in the dark.
Listening to the hard drive spin.
You wonder sometimes if you're wasting your life on a thing no one will ever use.
Then you open the laptop again.
You don't know what else to do with this feeling.
Because what you're building must never have an owner.
Inside the block you embed a headline from that morning's Times of London.
Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks.
For two months, you are the only person on Earth running the software.
You mine block after block, alone, writing transactions into a ledger that nobody else is reading, for a currency that nobody else believes in.
It's like writing letters to someone who hasn't been born yet.
A cryptographer who downloaded the software the day it went live.
He didn't ask what it was worth.
He didn't ask who you were.
He just wanted to see if it worked.
Months pass, and people start using it.
They don't even know you exist.
A few hundred users become a thousand, become ten thousand, and the more it grows, the less anyone needs to know who you are.
You answer questions on the forum sometimes at 2 AM.
Polite, patient, careful with every word.
Never too much personality, never too little.
You're building a voice that can disappear one day without anyone noticing the silence.
You watch the network grow from your screen like watching something breathe for the first time.
And that's exactly what will erase you.
A programmer in Florida buys two pizzas for ten thousand coins.
Years from now those coins will be worth hundreds of millions of dollars.
Because nobody realizes what they're holding.
Intelligence agencies are reading the forums now.
You try to explain once on the forum why the attention is dangerous.
Your hands hover over the keyboard longer than usual before you type it.
You didn't build this to become a headline.
You built it to become invisible.
But you can't control what free people do with a free tool you gave them for nothing.
And here's the thing you understand now.
If you stay, you're the center.
If you're the center, it isn't decentralized.
If it isn't decentralized, it's just another system run by another person with a name at the top.
You stare at the screen for a long time.
You crack your knuckles without thinking.
The only way your invention survives is if you don't.
You give them to a developer named Gavin Andresen.
He's the closest thing this project has to a successor.
You don't tell him that after today, you won't be answering emails anymore.
That you won't be fixing bugs at midnight, or reviewing patches in the morning, or answering strangers on the forum who still think you're just another developer.
That this is the last time anyone will hear from you.
You email a developer named Mike Hearn.
You write: I've moved on to other things.
Three days later, you email Gavin.
You write: I wish you wouldn't keep talking about me as a mysterious, shadowy figure.
Give more credit to the open source contributors.
You sit there after you send it.
He announces he's been invited to present Bitcoin at the CIA.
Your fingers stop moving on the keyboard.
For half a second they just hover there.
You just sit there a little longer than usual.
With the thing you spent four years building alone in a room nobody knows about.
You watch from a cafe somewhere.
Your coins - all one million of them - sit exactly where you left them.
You are the richest person in the world.
On a paper that doesn't have your name on it.
You file your taxes under a name that has nothing to do with any of this.
You see a doctor who doesn't know who you are.
You pay for groceries in cash.
You date someone once, maybe twice, and they ask what you do for work.
You say something about freelance software.
You finish your drink and go home alone.
And something in that relief makes you feel small.
The world keeps trying to find you.
Every few years, someone becomes you.
A retired engineer in California gets ambushed by reporters on his front lawn.
He says he's never heard of Bitcoin.
You wonder if that's what you'd look like if they found you.
A man in Australia goes to court swearing under oath that he invented it all, filing patents and lawsuits and press conferences.
And every time someone claims your name you let them take it.
Because the name was never the point.
The first person who ever received your coins.
You can't even post a single message saying goodbye.
Because the moment you speak you destroy everything you built.
So you sit somewhere quiet, and you remember him alone.
The way you do everything now.
In arguments at dinner tables between people who've never read the whitepaper, who've never written a line of code, who use your invention without knowing it has an inventor.
Governments that ignored you are now writing laws about you.
And the person who created it is nowhere.
Someone at the next table says Bitcoin is the future.
Your hands are warm around the mug.
The price is higher than yesterday.
I wonder where Satoshi is, he says.
The price of creating digital gold.
Is that you can never claim it as yours.
